skill.name: matplotlib
skill.description: Guidance and helper scripts for creating Matplotlib visualizations, including common plot types, styling, subplots, 3D plots, and export workflows.
skill.summary: Create clear Matplotlib plots and publication-ready figures.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many external command, cryptography, reconnaissance, credential, and network patterns. Review found these are Markdown code spans, plotting examples, documentation URLs, style labels, or argparse text, with no evidence of network calls, subprocess execution, secret access, obfuscation, or prompt injection. The skill includes helper scripts that can write local plot and style files, so overall risk is low and publication is acceptable with a local-file-write warning.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled Local Output Files
low_findings.description: The bundled helper scripts write plot or style output to a user-selected local path. This is expected for a Matplotlib utility, but users should avoid running it with sensitive or unintended output paths because existing files could be overwritten.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write behavior is directly visible through open(filename, w) and plt.savefig(args.output). The behavior is local and user-directed, with no evidence of exfiltration or command execution.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Representative static hits are Markdown backticks, Python plotting examples, style names, documentation URLs, and event-handler examples. They do not show Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, credential access, or system reconnaissance in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of cited locations shows ordinary documentation and plotting code rather than security-sensitive operations. The large count appears caused by Markdown inline-code parsing rather than executable shell syntax.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Write Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts save generated Matplotlib figures and style files to local paths. This is a normal plotting workflow, but it is still a filesystem side effect that users should understand before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both locations directly perform local writes through Python file APIs or Matplotlib savefig. No related network or shell execution path was found.

content.value_statement: Building clear scientific plots often requires many small Matplotlib decisions. This skill provides focused guidance, examples, and helper scripts for reliable figures.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use pyplot and object-oriented Matplotlib APIs.
Provides examples for line, scatter, bar, histogram, heatmap, contour, box, violin, and 3D plots.
Covers figure styling, rcParams, color maps, annotations, legends, and layout controls.
Includes helper scripts for plot templates and custom style-sheet generation.
Documents export settings for PNG, PDF, SVG, transparent backgrounds, DPI, and tight bounding boxes.
limitations: It does not validate user datasets or choose statistically correct charts automatically.
The helper scripts require a local Python environment with Matplotlib and NumPy installed.
Some examples may require optional libraries such as SciPy for fitted distributions.
Interactive and GUI behavior depends on the user environment and Matplotlib backend.

use_cases.title: Prepare Research Figures
use_cases.description: Create labeled, high-resolution figures with clear axes, legends, and export settings for papers or reports.

use_cases.title: Build Analysis Dashboards
use_cases.description: Combine several plots into clean subplot layouts that compare distributions, trends, and categories.

use_cases.title: Standardize Visual Style
use_cases.description: Generate reusable Matplotlib style settings for consistent presentation, web, publication, or dark-theme outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Plot
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Matplotlib line plot for my data. Include labels, a title, a legend, and a saved PNG output.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Right Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this dataset and goal, recommend the best Matplotlib chart type. Explain the choice and list the key plot settings.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Matplotlib figure and improve readability, colors, labels, legend placement, layout, and export quality.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Panel Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a publication-ready Matplotlib figure with multiple panels, shared axes where useful, consistent styling, annotations, and vector export.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a plot comparing monthly revenue and expenses.
output_examples.output: A clear recommendation for a line chart with two series, axis labels, a legend, grid styling, and export settings for presentation use.

output_examples.input: A researcher needs four related plots in one figure.
output_examples.output: A multi-panel layout plan with subplot structure, shared labels, consistent colors, spacing guidance, and publication export advice.

output_examples.input: A developer wants a reusable visual theme.
output_examples.output: A style configuration approach that defines fonts, figure size, grid behavior, line widths, and save settings for consistent plots.

best_practices: Use the object-oriented Figure and Axes API for reusable and maintainable plots.
Set figure size, layout, labels, and export resolution intentionally for the target medium.
Use accessible color maps, readable labels, and legends that do not hide data.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on implicit pyplot state for complex or production figures.
Do not use rainbow color maps for ordered numeric data.
Do not save outputs over important files without checking the destination path.

faq.question: Does this skill generate charts directly?
faq.answer: It provides guidance and helper scripts. The user still runs Python code in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it make publication-quality figures?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers DPI, vector formats, tight layouts, labels, legends, and object-oriented Matplotlib patterns.

faq.question: Does it support many plot types?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents common plot types, including line, scatter, bar, histogram, heatmap, contour, box, violin, and 3D plots.

faq.question: Are there security concerns?
faq.answer: The reviewed scripts can write local output files. No network calls, command execution, secret access, or prompt injection evidence was found.

faq.question: What dependencies are needed?
faq.answer: The examples require Python with Matplotlib and NumPy. Some optional examples may need SciPy.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
